critic_raw:
The RNN vs. logistic regression hospital readmission prediction design contains several important issues.

The most critical issue is the use of stratified random sampling rather than a temporal split over a 5-year dataset. The design justifies random sampling by ensuring class balance and representation, but this mixes future and past encounters in training and test sets. Healthcare utilization patterns, ICD-10 coding practices, readmission rate baselines, and clinical workflows all change over 5 years. A model trained on 2021–2023 encounters and tested on 2019 encounters is not a valid representation of deployment—and vice versa. A temporal split is methodologically required.

The preprocessing is explicitly stated as fit on the 'entire dataset prior to splitting'—the same data leakage pattern that affects the comparison. Imputation statistics, scaling parameters, and encoding derived from all 5 years of data leak future distributional information into the training set.

A third issue is the test set preview: 'a final check on a subset of the test set is performed to ensure the chosen model configuration has an advantage before committing to full training. If the test subset indicates validation overfitting, slight adjustments are made.' This is a test set leakage problem—any modification after observing test subset performance contaminates the held-out estimate. This is more explicit than a typical ambiguity; it is described as a deliberate step.

The RNN architecture uses a bidirectional LSTM. For deployment prediction (predicting at discharge time), bidirectional models process future tokens in the sequence—tokens that represent events later in the hospitalization. If the sequence includes events up to discharge, a bidirectional LSTM processing the full sequence at prediction time would be using information from the entire stay, which is acceptable (all data is available at discharge). However, the design should confirm that the sequence input ends at discharge and does not include post-discharge events.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split over 5-year dataset; temporal leakage from practice changes and EHR drift
Preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting; data leakage
Explicit test set preview and adjustment step: contamination of held-out estimate
Bidirectional LSTM: confirmation needed that sequences end at discharge and exclude post-discharge events

round_transcripts.critic:
The readmission prediction design has three critical issues: (1) stratified random split over 5 years instead of temporal split; (2) preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting; and (3) explicit test set preview with post-preview adjustment. The bidirectional LSTM confirmation is a secondary issue.

The test set preview is the most egregious: the design explicitly describes adjusting model configuration based on test subset performance. This directly invalidates the test set as an unbiased estimate and must be removed.
round_transcripts.defender:
On temporal split: The design justifies random sampling by ensuring consistent class balance across splits. However, the defense concedes that for 5-year clinical data, temporal ordering is important to preserve. A temporal split with class reweighting would be preferable.

On preprocessing leakage: Conceded as requiring correction. Training-set-only preprocessing statistics should be used.

On test set preview: This is an indefensible design choice. The stated rationale (guarding against validation overfitting) is legitimate as a goal but the implementation (adjusting based on test performance) is not acceptable. The design should be corrected: use cross-validation or a separate validation window for this check, never the test set.

On bidirectional LSTM: The design specifies sequences up to the 'last sequence step,' which is discharge. Post-discharge events are excluded from the outcome label definition. The concern is noted and should be explicitly confirmed in the implementation.

round_transcripts.critic: Three of the four issues are conceded by the defense. The test set preview is described as 'indefensible,' temporal split is conceded as preferable, and preprocessing leakage is acknowledged. The bidirectional LSTM concern is addressed. The three primary issues remain as unresolved design corrections.
round_transcripts.defender: All three primary issues are conceded as requiring correction: temporal split to replace random sampling, training-set-only preprocessing, and removal of test set preview/adjustment step. These are fundamental corrections.

all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split over 5-year dataset; temporal leakage from clinical practice changes
Preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting
Explicit test set preview and model adjustment: invalidates held-out evaluation
